data.title: Indonesia bowler enters record books with five-wicket over in T20 International
data.description:
Indonesia’s Gede Priandana became the first bowler to claim ‌five wickets in an over in a ‍Twenty20 International, in a game against Cambodia in Bali on Tuesday.
The 28-year-old seamer single-handedly derailed Cambodia’s chase in a ⁠seven-ball over that included a wide, completing his hat-trick and securing his team’s victory by 60 runs in the series opener.
Cambodia were 106 for five needing ‍168 for victory when Priandana was called on to ‍bowl the 16th over.
The right-armer dismissed Shah Abrar Hussain, Nirmaljit Singh and Chanthoeun ‌Rathanak in successive deliveries to complete his hat-trick ‍and followed up with a dot ball.
Priandana then removed Mongdara Sok and Pel Vennak with the last two legitimate deliveries of ‌his over, ⁠separated by a wide, to bundle out Cambodia for 107.
“Today Indonesia showed two things at once: the ability to execute games and win, and the ability to produce world-class moments,” Abhiram Singh Yadav, the Cricket Indonesia chairman, told BBC Sport.
“Five wickets in one over is a symbol of discipline, courage and the training standards we continue to build.
“This is not the finish line. This is a sign that Indonesian cricket is levelling up.”
Bangladesh’s Al-Amin Hossain and Indian Abhimanyu Mithun had claimed five wickets in an over in ‌domestic T20 matches.
There are 14 instances of bowlers claiming four wickets in an over ‌in international ‌matches, including Sri Lankan Lasith Malinga’s four in four balls against New Zealand in a T20 in 2019.

data.title: China’s reusable rocket ambitions suffer setback, US blacklists DJI: SCMP daily highlights
data.description:
Catch up on some of SCMP’s biggest China stories of the day. If you would like to see more of our reporting, please consider subscribing.
1. Second reusable rocket recovery failure in a month puts China 10 years behind US
The Long March 12A, China’s first state-owned reusable rocket, made its debut launch on Tuesday morning but the recovery of the first stage was a failure.
2. ‘Urgent’: has mainland China changed tone for Taiwan peaceful reunification?
Analysts examine Taiwan-related policy announcements, a new commemoration day and military posturing for clues to Beijing’s intent.
3. US adds new models of China’s DJI and all other foreign drones to its blacklist

The US telecommunications regulator said it is adding Shenzhen-based DJI, the world’s largest drone maker, to a list of companies determined to pose “unacceptable risks” to US national security, marking a significant escalation in Washington’s effort to crack down on Chinese drones.
4. China says US broke international law by seizing oil tankers off Venezuela
China accused the United States on Monday of violating international law by seizing oil tankers off Venezuela, as Washington intensified pressure on President Nicolas Maduro’s government and a senior US official openly suggested that he should be removed from power.
5. What China’s global yuan push looks like – and what could hold it back

The yuan has strengthened in recent months, with the offshore rate hitting its highest level against the US dollar in more than a year, fuelling forecasts that it could reach as high as 6.8 in 2026.
6. China drafts Antarctica activities and environment law to protect interests
China has drafted a law on Antarctic affairs, as Beijing looks to protect its interests and strengthen its role in the governance of the southernmost continent.
7. Fringe view of China’s history sparks official rebuke after going viral online

A wave of online discussion blaming China’s last imperial dynasty for national historical sufferings has prompted official criticism after touching on sensitive questions, and ignited debate over historical narrative, nationalism and social governance.

data.title: Soft rock duo Air Supply to headline Hong Kong’s New Year countdown celebrations
data.description:
Australian soft rock duo Air Supply will headline Hong Kong’s New Year countdown after the fireworks show was cancelled, with authorities expecting more than 11 million travellers to cross the city’s borders during the festive period.
The government also said on Tuesday that they were in discussions with the Shenzhen government to extend border crossing hours on New Year’s Eve, pending approval from the national port authorities.
The Hong Kong Tourism Board announced on Tuesday that Air Supply will perform at the countdown event on December 31, alongside Cantopop singers Jay Fung and Cloud Wan.
“The event will feature a brand-new format combining live performances by renowned singers and a light show, aiming to spread positive energy and heartfelt blessings,” it said.
The 40-minute event will take place at the Chater Road pedestrian zone in Central.
The facades of eight buildings in the business district will also be illuminated with immersive light projections of a giant countdown clock and festive patterns, forming a three-minute light display themed “New Hopes, New Beginnings”.
Last week, the board announced that the New Year’s Eve fireworks display at Victoria Harbour would be cancelled but did not give a reason for the decision.

The city is marking Christmas and New Year in a subdued mood following last month’s Tai Po fire, which killed 161 people.
Many large-scale events have been postponed or scaled down, prompting residents and visitors to celebrate in quieter ways amid a sombre atmosphere.
Dr Lam Ching-choi, chair of the Advisory Committee on Mental Health, previously said Hongkongers needed to return to normality at some point, adding that festivities should continue in a respectful manner.
The government on Tuesday also projected that around 11.52 million residents and visitors will pass through Hong Kong’s sea, land and air borders during the Christmas and New Year period.
The Immigration Department estimated that about 9.65 million passengers will cross through land border checkpoints, with peaks of around 689,000 travellers expected on Christmas Day and 695,000 on December 28.
It added that traffic at crossings linking Hong Kong with Shenzhen is expected to be heavy, with Lo Wu anticipated to record the highest daily average of 225,000 travellers.

data.title: Jack Ma-backed YF Capital takes stake in Shanghai memory chipmaker InnoStar
data.description:
YF Capital, a private equity firm backed by Chinese billionaire Jack Ma Yun, has taken a minority stake in Shanghai-based memory chip developer InnoStar Semiconductor, according to corporate records.
Hainan Yunfeng Fund Centre, an affiliate of YF Capital, became the chipmaker’s 14th-largest shareholder after acquiring a 2.1 per cent stake, based on a change record dated December 17 on business information platform Aiqicha.
The investment came after Ant Group backed InnoStar in August. Both YF Capital and Ant were founded by Ma, who also founded Chinese e-commerce giant Alibaba Group Holding. Alibaba owns the South China Morning Post.
Other new InnoStar shareholders included Real Power Capital, an investment firm backed by the Shanghai municipal government, which held 0.4 per cent, and Hong Kong-based Weihua Electronics, with 0.2 per cent, the filing showed.
YF Capital did not immediately respond to a request for comment on Tuesday.
InnoStar – a domestic developer of resistive random access memory (ReRAM) chips, a type of non-volatile memory that offers lower power consumption and faster data retention – was founded in 2019, according to public information.

The company was also the first in China to produce ReRAM chips using a 28-nanometre process technology.
Shanghai Alliance Investment, owned by the Shanghai municipal government, remained InnoStar’s biggest shareholder with a 16.6 per cent stake, followed by Hong Kong-incorporated Memris Asia-Pacific, with 15.6 per cent, Aiqicha data showed.
TikTok owner ByteDance last year invested in InnoStar through affiliates Picoheart and Beijing Weilai Yinji Technology, acquiring a combined 13.2 per cent shareholding.
A ByteDance representative at the time said the investment was made “in hopes of utilising the memory chips produced by InnoStar in Pico’s VR headsets”.
Capital’s move highlighted growing investor interest in semiconductors as mainland-designed chips are increasingly viewed as strategically important amid US technology restrictions.
The deal also came as the memory market entered an upcycle, with prices rising and prompting smartphone brands including Xiaomi and Honor to lift prices for some phones and tablets.

data.title: US releases thousands of new Epstein-linked documents
data.description:
Thousands of new documents linked to the case of convicted sex offender Jeffrey Epstein were made available Tuesday by the US Department of Justice (DOJ), amid mounting criticism over the pace of the publication and heavy redactions.
At least 8,000 files were posted online, including hundreds of videos or audio recordings, notably surveillance footage from August 2019, the month Epstein was found dead in his jail cell while awaiting trial on sex trafficking charges.
The DOJ posted around 11,000 links to new documents online, but some of them appeared to lead nowhere.
The Epstein Files Transparency Act (EFTA), passed nearly unanimously by Congress and signed by President Donald Trump, mandated the complete release of the Epstein files by Friday of last week.
Deputy Attorney General Todd Blanche has blamed the delay on the need to redact the identities of Epstein’s more than 1,000 victims from the hundreds of thousands of documents and photos in the government’s possession.

EFTA co-sponsors Ro Khanna, a Democrat, and Thomas Massie, a Republican, threatened over the weekend to bring contempt of Congress charges against Attorney General Pam Bondi for failing to comply with the law.
Democratic Senate Minority Leader Chuck Schumer introduced a resolution on Monday calling for legal action against the Trump administration for failing to release the complete Epstein files.
“Instead of transparency, the Trump administration released a tiny fraction of the files and blacked out massive portions of what little they provided,” Schumer said in a statement.
“This is a blatant cover-up. Pam Bondi and Todd Blanche are shielding Donald Trump from accountability, and the Senate has a duty to act.”
Blanche denied on Sunday that the Justice Department was redacting the files to protect Trump, a one-time close friend of Epstein.

“We are not redacting information around President Trump,” Blanche, a former personal lawyer to the president, told NBC’s “Meet the Press”.
Blanche said one picture of Trump was briefly removed over concerns for victims. It was later reposted after the Justice Department determined there was “no evidence that any Epstein victims are depicted”.
Trump initially tried to block the disclosure of the files linked to Epstein, a wealthy financier with rich and powerful connections.
The president, who cut ties with Epstein years before his arrest and faces no accusations of wrongdoing, finally bowed to mounting pressure from Congress and signed the law forcing publication of the files.
The sweeping blackouts across many of the documents – combined with tight control over the release by officials in Trump’s administration – have stoked scepticism over whether the disclosure will silence conspiracy theories of a high-level cover-up.
The tranche of materials released on Friday included photographs of former Democratic president Bill Clinton and other famous names, such as pop stars Mick Jagger and Michael Jackson, who were in Epstein’s social circle.
Clinton, in a statement released by his spokesman Angel Urena, urged the Justice Department to release any materials in the files related to the former president, saying he had nothing to hide.
“Someone or something is being protected. We do not know whom, what or why. But we do know this: we need no such protection,” Clinton said.
“The Department of Justice’s actions to date are not about transparency, but about insinuation using selective releases to imply wrongdoing about individuals who have already been repeatedly cleared by the very same Department of Justice,” he added.
Ghislaine Maxwell, Epstein’s former girlfriend, remains the only person convicted in connection with his crimes and is serving a 20 year sentence for recruiting underage girls for the former financier.

data.title: Luxury model built with Huawei outsells Porsche’s Panamera and BMW’s 7 series in China
data.description:
Chinese electric vehicles (EVs), already the envy of the global automotive industry, have raced to the top of the luxury segment after a new model co-developed by Huawei Technologies outsold Porsche’s Panamera and BMW’s 7 series.
Maextro S800, a luxury sedan priced from 708,000 yuan (US$100,555) to 1 million yuan, recorded 2,145 deliveries in mainland China last month, more than the combined sales of the Panamera and the 7 series, according to data provider ECC Intelligence.
Jointly developed by telecommunication equipment maker Huawei and state-owned JAC Group, the S800 is now the mainland’s top-selling car in the 700,000 yuan-plus price segment.
It had held the top spot for three consecutive months since September, when monthly deliveries hit 1,900 units, ECC data showed. Deliveries remained steady in October.
In November, Panamera sold 1,200 units and the 7 series recorded 928 deliveries.
“The brand’s rosy performance could bolster Chinese carmakers’ confidence in building more expensive electric cars to challenge the international marques like Mercedes and BMW,” said Tian Maowei, a sales manager at Yiyou Auto Service in Shanghai. “Chinese-made super luxury models still need to prove their quality and sustainability in the coming years as they attract more wealthy customers.”

The S800 debuted in late May, offering both pure electric and extended-range editions to mainland clients.
Before its arrival, China’s luxury car segment was dominated by international marques producing mainly petroleum-powered vehicles.
Chinese EV makers, from BYD to Leapmotor, have beaten foreign brands in the mass market, where consumers sought intelligent models priced below 150,000 yuan.
Big-name brands from Volkswagen to General Motors have lost market share to Chinese rivals over the past three years due to a slow progress in developing electric cars.
Currently, about 60 per cent of new cars sold to mainland customers were either battery-powered or plug-in hybrid models, according to the China Passenger Car Association.
At the end of 2024, UBS forecast that international carmakers would lose up to US$20 billion in annual profit on the mainland amid a rapid market shift towards electric cars and smarter vehicles.
Diminished demand for foreign brands would lead to an over­capacity of 10 million vehicles, Paul Gong, head of China automotive research at the Swiss bank, said at the time. He predicted, however, that foreign carmakers would remain competitive in the premium market because of their strong technological strength and brand awareness.
In 2023, BYD, the world’s largest electric-car builder, launched its first ultra-luxury model Yangwang U8, priced at 1.1 million yuan, in a bid to move up the value chain and challenge the likes of Range Rover.
Huawei entered the automotive business in 2019, focusing on technologies such as electrification, autonomous driving and digital cockpit. The company has been reiterating its role as a supplier of vehicle technology and systems, rather than a car builder, aiming to support assemblers worldwide in the transition to electrification and digitalisation.
Earlier this year, Huawei partnered with Shanghai-based SAIC Motor to develop smart EVs under a new brand as the country’s largest state-controlled carmaker sought to reverse six years of sliding sales in the world’s largest auto market.

data.title: China probes museum art theft claims that ‘make the Louvre thieves look dumb’
data.description:
The Chinese authorities are investigating reports that cultural treasures from a leading state-run museum were secretly sold on the market by museum staff in an alleged scam that has been described as making the Louvre thieves look “dumb”.
The scandal first came to light when a 16th-century painting that had been donated by the family of an art collector appeared for auction earlier this year.
A former director of the museum has since come under scrutiny after a former underling claimed that he had masterminded the large-scale theft and smuggling of national treasures.
The scale of the alleged crimes prompted one social media commentator to compare it with the recent heist in Paris where thieves disguised as construction workers stole some of the country’s crown jewels.
“Looks like the thief who stole from the Louvre still has a lot to learn – their method is just way too dumb,” the post said.
The allegations have shaken public trust in one of China’s leading museums at a time when the authorities are trying to promote the country as a cultural superpower and have prompted wider questions about the way cultural treasures are handled.
Nanjing Museum, founded in 1933 as the National Central Museum, is one of China’s first and most important museums.
It played a critical custodial role during World War II, helping to safeguard a vast portion of the Palace Museum collection when it was evacuated from Beijing following the Japanese invasion en route to a safer location – a history that helped establish its legacy as a guardian of national treasures.
The controversy first gained national attention when a Ming dynasty (1368-1644) painting, Spring in Jiangnan by Qiu Ying, appeared in a Beijing auction house catalogue in May with an estimated value of 88 million yuan (US$12.5 million).
The painting was part of a 137-piece collection donated in 1959 by the family of renowned collector Pang Laichen, and was the subject of a lawsuit filed by his descendants last year to find out what had happened to the paintings.

The artwork was one of five found to be missing during a court-ordered inventory check, prompting Pang’s great-granddaughter, Pang Shuling, to report the case to the authorities and ask the museum to provide documentation about the treatment of the artwork.
The painting was also withdrawn from sale after protests from the family.
Last week, the museum told state news agency Xinhua that the artwork, along with four others from the Pang family’s donation, had been certified as forgeries in 1961 and 1964.
The museum said the work had been formally removed from the collection in 1997 and transferred to a provincial cultural relics store, where it was sold for 6,800 yuan in 2001 to an unknown buyer.
It remains unclear how it made its way to the auction house with such a high price tag this year.
Pang Shuling has challenged the museum’s account, pointing to inconsistencies in its evidence and complaining about the museum’s lack of communication with her family.
In an interview with media outlet Caixin, Pang requested the return of the five alleged forgeries and two other artworks, citing breach of trust and improper treatment by Nanjing Museum.
She also urged the authorities to ensure the rest of the collection was managed properly.
“What this reveals is the loophole in how museums oversee their collections, and I hope the relevant government departments will pay attention to this,” she said.
“The country should improve the management system for museum collections, so that the public collections truly belong to everyone and are accessible to all.”
A former director of the museum, Xu Huping, 82, denied involvement in the sale of the painting, telling media he knew nothing of the matter and was “not a painting authentication expert”.
The case took a more dramatic turn on Sunday, when an 80-year-old retired employee from the museum, Guo Lidian, publicly accused Xu of the large-scale theft and smuggling of national treasures.
In a video statement, Guo also alleged that at some point in the early 2000s, Xu had illegally opened boxes that contained more than 100,000 treasures from the Palace Museum that had been stored in Nanjing since the end of the war.

Guo also said Xu had orchestrated a scheme to have authentic pieces falsely certified as replicas, then sold them at low prices to a provincial store he oversaw – the same store that had sold Spring in Jiangnan – before reselling them domestically and overseas.
Guo also said more than 40 colleagues had filed reports about Xu’s behaviour since he stepped down in 2008, but these had been ignored.
Xu has yet to respond to Guo’s accusations.
The spat has become a viral topic, sparking public concerns about the management of national treasures at the museum and beyond, as China has been heavily investing in museums and cultural initiatives in recent years to bolster national pride and the rejuvenation of traditional culture.
The National Cultural Heritage Administration announced on Tuesday that it has formed a special working group to investigate the museum’s handling of cultural treasures.
The Jiangsu provincial authorities have also established a cross-departmental investigation team to investigate the handling of artefacts, while the museum said it was conducting its own investigations.
On Thursday, the investigation teams have pledged to punish any violations of laws and regulations severely and promised to keep the public updated about the progress of the investigations.
China has been building a modern cultural heritage management system to better protect its national treasures following decades where they had been at risk from war, theft and smuggling.
Effective from March, China’s newly revised Cultural Relics Protection Law for the first time grants the country the permanent right to reclaim stolen or illegally exported cultural relics lost overseas.

data.title: Russian ally Belarus is courting both the US and China
data.description:
As Russia remains bogged down in Ukraine, Belarus – the Kremlin’s only ally in Europe – seeks to improve relations with the United States. Unwilling to remain completely dependent on its giant neighbour, Belarusian President Alexander Lukashenko seems to be attempting to revive his well-known “multi-vector” foreign policy, balancing between Moscow, the West and China. But will he succeed?
As the year nears its end, the 71-year-old Belarusian strongman has made several “humanitarian” gestures aimed at mending ties with the Trump administration. In early March, he openly praised US President Donald Trump and backed his approach to the war in Ukraine. A few months later, in June, he released a group of political prisoners, including Siarhei Tsikhanouski, the husband of exiled Belarusian opposition leader Sviatlana Tsikhanouskaya.
In August, Lukasheno spoke with Trump by phone, discussing bilateral ties, regional affairs and the war in Ukraine. A month later, he freed another group of political prisoners, including 14 foreign nationals and prominent opposition figure Mikola Statkevich. But unlike other detainees, Statkevich reportedly refused to be deported to Lithuania and was returned to prison. That, however, did not affect the warming of relations between the Belarusian leader and Washington.
In September, following a meeting with Lukashenko, Trump representative John Coale announced that the US might soon open its embassy in Minsk, normalise ties with Belarus and revive the economic and trade relationship between the two nations.
His statement was a clear indication that Washington plans to ease the restrictions it imposed on the former Soviet republic following controversial presidential elections in 2020 and Minsk’s decision to indirectly participate in the Russian invasion of Ukraine in 2022.
This month, following another meeting with Lukashenko, Coale declared that the US would lift sanctions on Belarusian potassium, the country’s key export. In exchange, Belarusian authorities released 123 political prisoners, including Nobel Peace Prize laureate Ales Bialiatski and key opposition figure Maria Kolesnikova.

Now that more than 430 critics of Lukashenko have been released since July 2024, Trump can undoubtedly cite this as a diplomatic victory. But that was unlikely his main goal. The closer ties Minsk develops with the US, the less dependent it will be on Russia. Consequently, in the long term, Belarus might join other former Soviet states that have effectively turned their back on Moscow and have shifted their geopolitical vector westward.
The Eastern European country, however, is still far from such developments. Under the current circumstances, Russia, more than any other foreign actor, has by far the most leverage in Belarus, despite Lukashenko’s attempts to flirt with other powers.
Before 2020, Lukashenko was seeking to develop closer ties with the West amid frequent political and economic disputes with Moscow. But following the West’s imposition of sanctions on Minsk in response to the crack down on protests that erupted after the vote, it was the Kremlin that openly supported the Belarusian strongman. As a result, Russia managed to strengthen its role in the neighbouring state.
Before the Russian invasion of Ukraine, Belarus – isolated from the West – was firmly in Moscow’s geopolitical orbit. The Biden administration did not even attempt to seriously undermine the Kremlin’s influence over the Eastern European country. Perhaps unsurprisingly, China was the first to exploit Moscow’s preoccupation with the conflict in Ukraine, significantly expanding its influence in Belarus.

Over the years, Beijing has succeeded in establishing strong economic ties with Minsk. China is now Belarus’ second-largest trading partner, while the former Soviet republic eyes expanded military cooperation with the world’s second-largest economy. A memorandum of understanding, signed between Belarus’ State Authority for Military Industry and China’s National Development and Reform Commission on December 14, is clearly a step in this direction.
For Lukashenko, China is a reliable strategic partner and a loyal friend of Belarus. Yet, his recent rhetoric, in which he applauds Trump’s efforts to freeze the conflict in Ukraine, suggests that he aims to restore his “multi-vector” foreign policy in which both Beijing and Washington would play a role, allowing Belarus to balance its relations and avoid overdependence on any single power.
“They say Trump likes flattery. But that is not my intention. I must say, I have been very impressed with his recent actions,” the Belarusian leader said on December 12.
Earlier this year, he used the same approach regarding Chinese President Xi Jinping, describing him as a leader who has “all the necessary qualities” and is “respected throughout the world”.

Lukashenko’s goal is clear: to reduce Belarus’ dependence on Russia while strengthening relations with both China and the United States. However, it remains unclear how the Trump administration – perceiving Beijing as its major geopolitical opponent – will react to such ambitions.
The problem for Trump is that the European Union – at least for now – does not seem willing to adopt Washington’s approach and normalise relations with Belarus. Instead, on December 15, the 27-nation bloc expanded sanctions against Minsk, accusing it of launching meteorological balloon incursions into the airspace of Lithuania.
Without the lifting of European sanctions, Belarus will have to continue relying on Russia and its ports to export goods. At the same time, cut off from Europe, Minsk will almost certainly continue developing closer ties with Beijing.
Therefore, for Trump, Belarus is likely to remain, at least for the foreseeable future, a lever in a game played simultaneously with China, the EU and Russia.

data.title: K-drama Love Me: Seo Hyun-jin leads slow-burn ensemble family drama with heavy emotions
data.description:
Lead cast: Seo Hyun-jin, Yoo Jae-myung, Chang Ryul
Latest Nielsen rating: 1.5 per cent
Loneliness, guilt and desire form the heart of Love Me, a melodrama that begins on a dour note before smoothing over the pain with the promise of new romance.
Although Seo Hyun-jin (The Trunk) gets the most screen time, this is at its core an ensemble piece about six characters: three members of a fractured family and the partners to which they slowly turn to mend their broken hearts.


This is the second series helmed by Jo Young-min this year, following the critically acclaimed Netflix drama You and Everything Else. The script is penned by writing duo Park Eun-young and Park Hee-kwon (I Have Not Done My Best Yet); Park Eun-young previously collaborated with Seo on The Trunk.
Seo stars as Seo Jun-kyung, an obstetrician-gynaecologist who has been living a lonely life filled with long shifts, after-work convenience store beers and disappointing blind dates ever since leaving her family home seven years earlier.
Her father, Seo Jin-ho (Yoo Jae-myung, Tempest), has just taken early retirement to better care for his physically disabled wife, Kim Mi-ran (Jang Hye-jin, Parasite). Still living at home is the college-age Seo Jun-seo (Lee Si-woo, Love Your Enemy).
Jun-kyung seldom returns home, and the mood is never festive when she does, as tensions quickly explode between mother and daughter.

The reason for these frosty relations is soon made clear: Mi-ran was incapacitated in a crash seven years ago while driving to Jun-kyung’s hospital on a rainy day to bring her something she had forgotten at home.
Jun-kyung’s guilt compelled her to leave home, and the family has never been able to repair the psychological damage caused by the accident.
Things go from bad to worse early on when tragedy strikes the family again. Yet from this low point, all that is left is for the remaining characters to rebuild, each in their own time and in their own way.
For Jun-kyung, this takes the form of her next-door neighbour Jo Du-hyun (Chang Ryul, Daily Dose of Sunshine), a free spirit who has quietly noticed her strength and hidden charms.

Jun-seo is already in a passionate relationship at the beginning of the series, but his girlfriend, a club entertainer, attracts a lot of attention, and jealousy starts to get the better of him.
Jun-seo’s friend, Ji Hye-on (Dahyun of K-pop group Twice), is quickly presented as an alternative love interest, though nothing sparks in the early episodes.
For Jin-ho, who has just become a widower, it may take longer to open up, but Jin Ja-young (Yoon Se-ah, Perfect Family) is cautiously introduced as a potential love interest in episode two.
With its weighty themes and their occasionally heavy-handed treatment, Love Me may not be everyone’s cup of tea. However, it largely achieves what it aims to, which is to say it succeeds as a tale of redemption, where romance is the saviour that pulls several characters back from the brink.

If the series suffers from any crucial flaw, it is the glaring age mismatch between some of its cast members. In real life, Seo is only 12 years younger than Yoo, who plays her father, and 10 years younger than Jang, who plays her mother, and the production does little to bridge that discrepancy.
Narrative television is make-believe, and actors are called upon to play characters of different ages, but suspension of disbelief can only take you so far. In Love Me, this creates a disconnect that at times makes it hard to get into the story.
Once we look past this issue, what we are left with is a show that is deliberate in its pacing, sensitive to the psychological needs of its characters, and honest about the flaws their wounds have woven into them.
The familial drama of the early episodes serves as a structural foundation from which three slow-burning romances can steadily rise. What is less clear yet is what kind of romantic series Love Me will turn into in the coming weeks.

Given the neediness of the title, there is a risk the narrative will focus squarely on the Seos and their emotional voids. As we learn more about their potential partners in the episodes ahead, hopefully the show will remember that love is a two-way street.
Love Me is streaming on Viu.

data.title: China vows city-specific fixes to clear housing glut, stabilise property sector
data.description:
China has pledged to empower local governments with city-specific measures to clear housing gluts and optimise supply next year, ramping up efforts to stabilise a real estate market that remains a persistent drag on the world’s second-largest economy.
“Municipal governments should fully utilise their autonomy in regulating the property market, adjusting and optimising policies as appropriate,” said Ni Hong, the minister of housing and urban-rural development, at a two-day work conference that ended on Tuesday.
While risk prevention remains the priority, the meeting served to emphasise that real estate “still has considerable development potential”, given the rising rate of urbanisation and people’s expectations for “quality homes”, according to an official readout.
To mitigate delivery risks in commercial housing, the ministry is pushing for a shift towards selling completed homes in a “what you see is what you get” model.
For projects that continue to operate on the traditional presales model – a key amplifier of the property crisis – Ni called for stricter oversight of funds to ensure that homebuyers’ rights are protected.
Authorities also pledged to support local governments in purchasing existing commercial housing for use as affordable units, resettlement housing and dormitories.
The ministry also declared that it had “fully accomplished” its campaign – enacted in 2022 – to revive stalled real estate projects and deliver new units in a timely manner.


The emphasis on selling finished homes marks a fundamental overhaul of the industry’s long-standing practice in which developers sold units long before completion, or sometimes before construction had begun. That business model unravelled in 2021 following the liquidity crisis of industry giant Evergrande, triggering a wave of developer defaults and stalled projects across the country.
Since then, China’s real estate sector, formerly a primary engine of economic growth, has been mired in a prolonged downturn. With considerable middle-class wealth tied up in depreciated properties, the slump has weighed heavily on consumption and reinforced deflationary pressures.
With 2026 marking the start of China’s 15th five-year planning period – which the ministry described as a “critical juncture of transformation and development” for housing – authorities are intensifying efforts to steady the sector.
However, some analysts warn that the decline will persist through next year.
China’s home prices continued to fall at a rapid pace in November, with tier-one cities, including Beijing, Guangzhou and Shenzhen, seeing new home prices fall 1.2 per cent from a year earlier, while resale home prices dropped 5.8 per cent.
The property sector is expected to produce a 1.5 percentage point drag on China’s GDP growth in 2026, according to a Goldman Sachs forecast on Friday.
